item.title: Have whatever opinion on veganism, but don't do shit like this.
item.description: The most important thing you can do with your life is not interfere with anyone else's life

item.title: Images of Note: 9.9 on the cunter scale
item.description:
Iceland.png
This kind of logic fucking concerns me because, fun fact, starving people aren't going to pull themselves up by their bootstraps because that is literally an analogy for something that is impossible. People need shelter and food, what is the point in civilization if those things are not readily accessible?
Listen, there is a difference between "looking pretty" and "having a nice outfit" and "serving cunt".
WE BALL FUCKER
nya :3c
Thing is though, Shadow the Hedgehog is not a Cosmic Horror
This is currently my favorite meme, the wilder the characters the better. Give me Toby from Thomas the Tank Engine and Dr T'Ana from Star Trek Lower Decks.
It's surprising how many people believe you "can't say Merry Christmas anymore".
Hell yeah
For Chats: http://discord.abbii.gay For Shitposts: http://tumblr.abbii.gay For srs and maybe shitposts: http://mastodon.abbii.gay
item.dc:creator: abbiistabbii

item.title: Michael Harriot gives some perspective on Nikki Haley
item.description:
With all the BS leading up to the election and her refusing to admit that slavery was the root cause of the American Civil War, Mr. Harriot retweeted this thread from June, and since I didn't post it then for some reason, here it is now. https://twitter.com/michaelharriot/status/1669915356524163073
https://www.theatlantic.com/national/archive/2012/12/in-southern-towns-segregation-academies-are-still-going-strong/266207/ https://www.history.com/topics/1960s/orangeburg-massacre
https://www.salon.com/2015/07/08/south_carolina_does_not_do_subtle_racism_my_life_inside_a_segregation_academy/
https://twitter.com/michaelharriot/status/1669915356524163073 There's never a bad time to remind people how important it is to vote in every election, especially the local ones, because that is the easiest way to make sure such evil people never get their foot in the door.
item.dc:creator: KalliJ

item.title: I promise I’m okay :)

First dump
Be nice
Or don’t
I’m not the cops
Some of these get sad
But that’s a risk I’m willing to take

item.title: Damned King
item.description:
Chaim Rumkowski (b.1877) was appointed Judenältester - Jewish Elder - of the Jewish ghetto established in the Polish city of Łódź by Hans Biebow (1902-1947), the Nazi supervisor put in place after the Nazi invasion of Poland and the subsequent annexation of the part of Poland containing Łódź into Germany. The reasons for his selection by the Nazi authorities are unclear, his pre-war career had been managing a children's home, and some time working in insurance. The city of Łódź had a population of about 225,000 Jews before the war, after the dislocations of the Nazi invasion, and the deporting of rural Jews into the ghetto, 164,000 people were crammed into an area of the city approximately 4 square kilometres (1.5 square miles) in size, of which 2.4 square kilometres (0.93 square miles) was habitable. Generally, in other Jewish Ghettos throughout Poland, the internal situation throughout the Second World War was one of confusion, terror, and some resistance. While many simply struggled to survive, secret Nazi collaborators vied with mafia-like criminal gangs and smugglers who vied with resistance groups often divided by political outlook (Nationalist, Zionist, Socialist/Communist, etc); with the Nazi-appointed 'authorities', the Jewish Police (Jupo) and Jewish Councils (Judenrats) secondary players - and sometimes, of course, these groups overlapped, with Nazi collaborators infiltrating resistance groups while working also for criminal gain, for example.
[Picture: Chaim Rumkowski and his "Cabinet", the Jewish Council - Judenrat. His original council, appointed on 16 October 1939, consisted of 31 well-known figures, but within weeks 20 were executed and the remainder 'disappeared' in still-mysterious circumstances, leaving Rumkowski the undisputed dictator of the tiny ghetto.] This was not the case in Łódź. Rumkowski realised that the ultimate aim of the Nazi regime towards Jews was some horror, it is unlikely he originally knew it was extermination, but the publicly-discussed idea of "resettlement" to the East, to labour as serfs until death, is hardly vastly different. Consequently Rumkowski took it upon himself to turn the Łódź ghetto into a valuable, productive part of the Reich; using the slogan "work is the only way". The Nazis took him up on this offer of work, but refused to pay money - instead, food supplies to the ghetto would be delivered in proportion to goods produced in the ghetto.
[Picture: Jews in Łódź ghetto remove the Jewish star from clothing sent back from Chelmo Extermination camp, the clothes would then be provided to Germans.] Already an industrial city, the tiny Łódź ghetto became home to 100 factories, primarily of textiles, which produced uniforms for the Nazi regime, but including some munitions works. Jewish hands stitched the little eagle-and-swastika that adorned the right breast of Nazi tunics. Later, as the Holocaust intensified, Łódź received deliveries of clothes that belonged to now-dead Jews, for the Nazi-demanded Star of David patches to be removed so the clothes could be used by Aryans. One workshop produced children's toys sold by the SS to German children. To facilitate this, Rumkowski ruled Łódź with an iron fist.
[Picture: the strong arm of Rumkowski - the Jewish Ghetto Police (Jupo), who numbered about 1,500.] Unlike in other Ghettos, which became hotbeds of desperate intrigue - such as in Warsaw, where criminal-gangs and collaborator groups like "Group 13" and the Żagiew gang operated relatively openly, including blackmailing in-hiding Jews to keep their locations secret, in Łódź control by Chaim Rumkowski, and the police he directly controlled, was near-total and enforced with a ruthlessness that led, both at the time, and today, to comparisons to the Hitler regime that empowered him. Rumkowski earnt the nickname "King Chaim" for this absolutism. His rule was enforced with the ultimate sanction - handing individuals over to the Nazis. Groups including communists, Zionists, and others briefly organised before Rumkowski's oppression crushed them - usually by the simple method of denying them work, which was a guarantee of starvation. Alongside this despotic rule, his administration maintained a network of medical facilities only undermined by a lack of resources allowed in by the Nazis, and maintained a schooling system that meant 63% of the children in the population remained in a form of formal schooling - a level unheard of in any other ghetto.
[Image: One of the three wire-enclosed bridges which reached over several important arterial streets carrying Lodz's tram tracks, which divided the ghetto but were inaccessible to Jews. Ghetto residents trudging across these bridges were in full view of local Poles and German occupation forces; this bridge is at Zgierska Street near Plac Koscielny.] Some opponents were handed over to the Nazis in an exemplary manner, and a few early protests in the ghetto that the Jupo could not handle led to Rumkowski requesting support from German troops, leading to deaths. By early 1941, organised resistance in the ghetto to Rumkowski's strategy was negligible, this control also had the effect of further sealing the ghetto off from the surrounding city. Other Jewish ghettoes were tenuously integrated into their surrounding city's black markets via smugglers, but Łódź was uniquely cut off by Rumkowski's hunting down of any alternative means of 'making do' without him, which included the issuing of a ghetto-only currency. One of the few ways to see life beyond the ghetto without being part of a Rumkowski-organised working detail was while crossing the three bridges the division of the ghetto by "Aryan-only" tram tracks made necessary, Sara Zyskind (1927-1995) movingly wrote in her book "The Stolen Years" how, following the death of her mother in the ghetto in 1940, and her father in 1943, she would look from the bridge to her parent's former home, now as far away as another planet, and remember when she had been happy.
[Picture: Rumkowski addressing a crowd in the Ghetto sometime in 1941-42, the church towers are of St Mary's Assumption, part of the ghetto, it was used as a storage area of clothing brought back from the Chelmo extermination camp.] Rumkowski cultivated a cult of personality around himself, heroic paintings (many by talented artist Izrael Lejzerowicz (1902-1944)) and propaganda posters of him beamed down from walls, his face appeared on the stamps of the ghetto's tiny postal service, children's plays were written that featured him as a delivering saviour, and the workshops he ruled competed with each other to give him the most lavish gifts - within the means of a sealed, desperate ghetto - on his birthday. Nonetheless, survivor's remember his viciousness and personal exploitation, particularly of women in his employ - and allegedly children - and that he frequently met any challenge with a reminder that he could hand anyone over to the Germans at any time. Rumkowski's strategy of making the Łódź ghetto a useful cog in the Nazi state came at great & bloody cost. Whether from his own inclinations, or over-identifying with the captors who gifted him such absolute power over his fellows, he divided his ghetto into the "productive" and the "useless eaters" (a Nazi term originally coined to refer to the mentally ill and disabled of Germany, as part of encouraging support for the euthanasia programme Aktion T4).
[Image: Rumkowski meets Heinrich Himmler (1900-1945) on 5 June 1941, during an official visit by the head of the SS to the Ghetto. Amongst the Nazi officials in this picture is Karl Wolff (1900-1984), who escaped post-war prosecution due to arranging the early surrender of German forces in Italy in 1945, but was arrested in 1962 when his involvement in the deportation of Polish Jews became more widely known, he was imprisoned until 1971.] Most infamously, on 4 September 1942, Chaim delivered what became known as the "Give me your children" speech. Despite Rumkowski's consistent forcing of all-hands work, including the employment of the children held in the Children's Concentration Camp attached to the ghetto (where Polish child "criminals", homeless children, and orphans were confined), the Nazis announced that the ghetto remained inefficient and had a poor cost-benefit ratio for the Reich - they therefore demanded the ghetto be reduced by 24,000 people. The decision-making process that followed is unclear, including if it was simply a directive from the Nazis, or if Rumkowski and the Judenrat had any input, but the end result was that Rumkowski announced to the ghetto - while heavily protected by the Jupo - that everyone over the age of 65 in the ghetto, all those unable to work due to disablement, and all children to the age of 10, were to be deported.
[Picture: Like Herod; all Jewish children under the age of 10 being deported.] He said: "A grievous blow has struck the ghetto. They [the Germans] are asking us to give up the best we possess – the children and the elderly. I was unworthy of having a child of my own, so I gave the best years of my life to children [before the war, Rumkowski had ran an orphanage]. I've lived and breathed with children. I never imagined I would be forced to deliver this sacrifice to the altar with my own hands [this feels like a reference to the story of Abraham & Isaac]. In my old age, I must stretch out my hands and beg: Brothers and sisters! Hand them over to me! Fathers and mothers: Give me your children!" He went on to explain how the Nazis had demanded the ghetto shrink by 24,000, but he had argued for a reduction to 20,000, which they accepted.
[Picture: two unidentified children in the ghetto play "Cops and Robbers" with one of the children dressed as a Jupo. This image is one of 3,000 saved by photographer Henryk Ross (1910-1991), the 'official photographer' of the Judenrat's Department of Statistics, who also secretly took photographs of the true conditions of the ghetto. He buried 6,000 negatives before deportation. Surviving the end of the ghetto, he returned to recover 3,000 that were not water damaged in January 1945.] Rumkowski continued: "A broken Jew stands before you. This is the most difficult of all orders I have ever had to carry out at any time. I reach out to you with my broken, trembling hands and beg: give into my hands the victims. So that we can avoid having further victims, and a population of 100,000 Jews can be preserved. So, they promised me: If we deliver our victims by ourselves, there will be peace. I understand what it means to tear off a part of the body. So which is better? What do you want? That 80,000 to 90,000 Jews remain, or God forbid, that the whole population be annihilated?"
[Picture: Lodz ghetto inmates making baskets.] Many families committed group suicide rather than be separated by this order, but 10,000 children - rounded up by the Jupo - went to their deaths at Chelmo Extermination Camp, with no parents to comfort them. Despite Rumkowski's speech, of roughly a further 10,000 people deported alongside the children, large numbers were able bodied and of working ages, having been rounded up at random by German soldiers, who entered the ghetto as the SS felt the Jupo-led deportation was too slow. Rumkowski's efforts to make the ghetto a productive part of the Reich were continually challenged by the SS, who repeatedly reported the ghetto was inefficient. This was despite the ghetto factories producing an estimated net profit of 35 million Reichsmarks, which was largely pocketed by the local Nazi leadership. This "inefficient" judgement seems to have been inevitable, the SS having, by late 1942, established that the 'Final Solution' would be one of mass, systematic, extermination, rather than a previous idea of gradual reduction in the Jewish population by working-to-death. The beginning of the end of the ghetto began in June 1944, with SS plans being put in place for a final 'liquidation'.
[Image: Rumkowski gives a speech, solidly protected by a phalanx of Jupo.] However, the Nazi Minister for War Production, Albert Speer (1905-1981), repeatedly resisted the destruction of Łódź ghetto, including speaking to Hitler directly, suggesting that Rumkowski's focus on production did have some genuine effect on the chaotic Nazi decision making process - albeit one of the few parts that followed some form of rational judgement, as compared to the fecund race-hate of the SS. A sudden pause in the liquidation in early July 1944 is assumed a result of Speer's intervention.
[Picture: "King Chaim" featured on a postage stamp for the ghetto postal service. This features one of the wire-enclosed bridges, which had became emblematic of ghetto to many residents.] Rumkowski's obsequious responses to Nazi demands meant that by 1944 Łódź was the largest remaining concentration of Jews in Poland outside of concentration camps, lasting a whole year longer than any similar sized ghetto; with Krakow's ghetto being "liquidated" in March 1943, Warsaw's ghetto between April and May 1943 (having already been vastly reduced between July-September 1942, during which Adam Czerniaków (1880-1942), Jewish Elder of Warsaw, committed suicide rather than collabroate), and Lwow ghetto in June 1943. The death knell of the Łódź ghetto came on 1 August 1944, the Polish underground army rose up in the Warsaw Uprising, only 65 miles away from Łódź. As the Nazis pulled troops back from fighting the encroaching Red Army to crush that uprising, resistance to the idea of liquidating Łódź also stopped.
[Picture: The Polish resistance in Warsaw operating a captured Panther tank in the Uprising. Polish workers forced to work in Nazi maintenance workshops had the necessary skills to keep these vehicles running.] The Soviet army, to the world's shock, did not launch an offensive to relieve the Polish resistance in Warsaw, for reasons that are debated widely to this day; but which I believe was simply a cynical move to allow the Nazis to eliminate the forces that might have supported an anti-Soviet Polish government, when the USSR already had a pet Polish government waiting for installation. It is also not often recalled that Stalin (1878-1953) disasterously disobeyed orders to transfer part of his forces to aid the army of Mikhail Tukhachevsky (1893-1937) at Warsaw in 1920, likely stopping Lenin's (1870-1924) revolution from reaching Berlin - and perhaps all of Europe - so Stalin had good & vicious reason to be disinclined to allow Warsaw to again prove a bulwark against him.
[Picture: Fit for a king; Chaim is driven around the ghetto in his personal horse-drawn carriage.] Because the USSR did not rush to link up with Polish forces in Warsaw, an opportunity to punch a hole in the centre of the Nazi line was lost, and Łódź would have been the inevitable next stop for the Red Army's advance. As the Warsaw Uprising struggled on against the Nazis until October 1944, with the Red Army resting and rearming just beyond Warsaw, the Jews of Łódź met their end, with the entire ghetto being cleared out by the end of August 1944. Only 200,000 (from 3 million) Polish Jews survived World War Two and the Holocaust.
[Image: Rumkowski's motto for the ghetto, on a wall in both German and Hebrew: "work is the only way". It is unclear if he was ever aware of the now-infamous gate at Auschwitz 1, which mockingly announced "work will set you free", a quote from the philsopher Lorenz Diefenbach (1806-1883).] At liquidation in August 1944, the Łódź ghetto still had roughly 80,000 people in it. It is an interesting counter-factual to imagine what history would now make of the pathetically powerless, yet balefully powerful, tyrant Chaim Rumkowski, both hideous dictator and doomed victim, if he'd been the man responsible for the survival of a third more of Poland's Jews than in reality. It is also likewise a hideous question as to what those 80,000 people would make of a life they gained by the sacrifice of their children and what traumas they would carry, having been forced to acquiesce to the obsequious Nazi collaboration & despotism of "King Chaim". Chaim Rumkowski was deported to Auschwitz in one of the final deportations of the now empty Łódź ghetto inhabitants. It is unclear if he travelled in any greater comfort or pomp due to his position. He did not make it to the gas chamber, being instead beaten to death by prisoners of Sonderkommando II (a prisoner work group who operated the gas chambers) on 28 August 1944. ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- If you like my writing, and desire a much more uplifting tale, it may interest you to know my first book, about the Colonial Marines - enslaved African-Americans who joined British forces in the War of 1812, and gained their freedom - is now available for pre-order: UK Amazon: https://www.amazon.co.uk/Black-Redcoats-Colonial-Marines-1814-1816/dp/1399034014/ US Amazon: https://www.amazon.com/Black-Redcoats-Colonial-Marines-1814-1816/dp/1399034014/
item.dc:creator: BodicaRexVII

item.dc:creator: merlinJumper

item.title: They keep trying to normalize not being able to afford a house
item.description: I bought a house in 2010, but the property year in 2009, I rented a 2 story house in good area for $800 a month. Balcony + Yard + Shed . Now in this area, you can't rent anything for less than 1500. Even apartments are like 1k+. Edit. for context. Minimum wage here in Texas is STILL. $7.25. I started working when I was 16 and it was 5.25 back then. I'm about to turn 40 and it's only gone up $2 in all those years while everything else has gone way up.
item.dc:creator: imthepatchmaker
